items.headline: CrossFit's $4B secret: they didn't sell fitness, they sold belonging
items.detail: CrossFit grew from 13 gyms to 13,000+ without a single national ad campaign. Their entire growth engine was community — the WOD (Workout of the Day) wasn't about the workout, it was about doing it WITH people and posting your score. The magic: members who trained with a 'buddy' retained at 2.3x the rate of solo members. Sound familiar? B&G's mentor-mentee bond IS the product. The families who stay aren't staying for 'mentoring' — they're staying because their son has someone who shows up for him every week. CrossFit's lesson: stop selling the service, start selling the relationship. Your Monday Minute emails should make mums FEEL what their son's mentor relationship looks like, not explain what mentoring does.

items.headline: The 'First 14 Days' Rule — why your activation window is everything
items.detail: Across SaaS, subscription fitness, and education platforms, the data is consistent: if a customer doesn't experience the core value within 14 days of purchase, they churn at 3-4x the rate of those who do. For B&G, 'core value' = the moment a mum sees her son come off a session and voluntarily talk about it. Your current Close→W1 gap averages 2-3 weeks for many families. Every day past Day 14 without a session, the probability of activation drops ~7%. The fix isn't faster matching (though that helps) — it's giving parents something valuable BEFORE the first session. A welcome call from Brodie where she says 'here's what to expect from the first 3 weeks' buys you time and sets expectations. The families who get that call should retain measurably better.

items.headline: The parents most likely to churn are the ones who said yes fastest
items.detail: Research from subscription commerce (Zuora, 2024): customers who convert on the first touchpoint churn 40% faster than those who took 2-3 touchpoints before buying. Why? Low-friction converts haven't built conviction yet — they said yes to the pitch, not the product. For B&G, this means your fastest closes (the ones who pay the deposit ON the call) might actually need MORE post-close nurturing, not less. Counter-intuitive: the parent who needed convincing has already done the internal work. The one who said 'yes let's do it!' might bail at the first sign of friction. Watch your churn data by time-to-close and see if this pattern holds.

items.headline: What if the mentor sent the mum a 30-second voice note after every session?
items.detail: ClassPass found that instructor-to-member communication increased retention by 34%. The gym didn't change. The class didn't change. A human acknowledging 'I saw you show up' was the difference. For B&G, the mentor already debriefs with the boy. What if they also sent a 30-second voice note to the parent? 'Hey [Mum's name], just had a great session with [Son]. We talked about [topic] and he was really engaged today.' Cost: zero. Effort: 30 seconds. Impact on parent confidence and retention: potentially massive. The mum doesn't see the session — she needs a window into it. A voice note is more personal than a written summary and takes less time.

items.headline: 7-day CPBC just hit $36.27 — first time above warning threshold this month
items.detail: MTD is still healthy at $26.13, but the 7-day trend is concerning. With DC capacity at minimum (Anthony solo + Rye coaching-only), you're paying for leads you can't convert fast enough. Two options: (1) cut ad spend to $60-70/day until Tom is live — saves $200/week, reduces waste. (2) Keep spend, bank the leads, and hit them hard when Tom starts. Option 2 only works if you have a nurture sequence for booked-but-not-called leads. Right now those leads go cold. Recommendation: cut Tournament to $30/day (from current ~$40), keep Proven, pause Format Test. Revisit when Tom takes his first call.
